title: 2026 CCMP
text:
WATER
WILDLIFE
WAY OF LIFE
2026 Revision to the Comprehensive Conservation and Management Plan for the Narragansett Bay Region

title: Preface
text:
This 2026 Revision to the Comprehensive Conservation and Management Plan (CCMP) for the Narragansett Bay region is a ten-year blueprint for collaborative action among partners of the Narragansett Bay Estuary Program (NBEP) to sustain and restore Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, the Coastal Salt Ponds, and their watersheds in Rhode Island (RI), Massachusetts (MA), and Connecticut (CT), hereafter “Narragansett Bay region.” The estuaries, rivers, streams, and watersheds of the Narragansett Bay region are home to diverse and vibrant habitats, wildlife, and human communities. This CCMP Revision aims to build upon conservation successes while addressing legacy and emerging threats to this beautiful landscape and its inhabitants.
NBEP is a catalyst for scientific inquiry and collective action to investigate, document, restore, and protect water quality, wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region. Since NBEP’s last CCMP Update (NBEP 2012), NBEP has intentionally broadened its geographic and organizational focus to emerge as a partnership-based, service-oriented organization rooted in science and dedicated to action throughout the 2,000 square mile Narragansett Bay region. The 113 actions in the 2012 CCMP have been consolidated into 24 priority opportunities for collaborative work. All 24 Actions arose from an intensive five-year period of input from NBEP committee members and other partners. Each Action has been carefully considered through four strategic lenses: regionalism, connectivity, resiliency, and fairness. In highlighting these strategic lenses, NBEP strives to acknowledge and center the Narragansett Bay region’s long, shared history of industrialization and habitat fragmentation that set the stage for current challenges. At the same time, NBEP looks to the strategic lenses for inspiration to rightfully restore a landscape that has provided so much to its residents, and to capitalize on the region’s strengths and opportunities to shape a lasting future for our water, wildlife, and ways of life.



2026 CCMP Revision Framework



Acknowledgements
The Narragansett Bay Estuary Program Steering, Executive, and Science Advisory Committees provide invaluable guidance, energy, and expertise for all collaborative endeavors, including this CCMP Revision. Thank you.
NBEP Staff (2026): Danielle Moore, Jennifer Rogers, Mariel Sorlien, Courtney Schmidt, and Darcy Young.
Thank you to the former NBEP staff members who shaped the process of this CCMP Revision: Julia Bancroft, Mike Gerel, and Julia Twichell.
Facilitators: Coastwise Partners
Writers and Editors: Shafer Consulting, LLC

title: Action People-3.2: Regional Targets
text:
Full Action Statement: Establish shared regional ecosystem targets with public reporting systems to track progress.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls for NBEP to lead the development of priority regional ecosystem targets for the Narragansett Bay region to motivate collective action.
Effective protection and restoration of water, habitats, fish and wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region depends on 1) understanding the status and trends of key environmental and socioeconomic indicators; 2) establishing measurable targets for those indicators that equate to a desired future condition; and 3) adopting reporting systems to monitor progress and foster additional collaborative work to reach targets.
In 2017, NBEP and partners identified and characterized the status and trends of 24 key stressor and condition indicators underlying the ecosystem health of Narragansett Bay and its communities (NBEP 2017). NBEP and partners will continue to develop indicators (Action: People-2.3) and monitor and model their status and trends (Actions: Water-1.1, Habitat-1.1, and People-2.2).
NBEP will collaborate with partners to develop targets at appropriate scales that define desired future conditions for indicators. Target development will take into consideration existing targets, such as those required by the Clean Water Act to support designated uses of waters, Total Maximum Daily Loads (TMDLs), Special Areas Management Plans (SAMPs), and habitat restoration plans. Targets should encompass topics that NBEP and partners have agency and capacity to advance and ideally derive from metrics that NBEP already measures for State of the Watershed indicators (e.g., habitat acres, pollution reductions, number of public access points).


Potential Projects

Convene partners to create numeric targets for priority ecosystem and socioeconomic system indicators that span watershed boundaries and authorities.
Establish reporting systems for tracking progress toward shared targets.



Outputs

Facilitated meetings
Numeric targets and reporting system(s) for priority environmental and socioeconomic indicators



Outcomes

Improved coordination and collaboration among protection and restoration partners
Increased public awareness of management goals and the effectiveness of actions and investments to attain them



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Synthesize and communicate science


Implementing Partners
Steering Committee


Location
Narragansett Bay region


Timeline for initiation
2026-2029


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$500K–1M
NBEP 320/SNEP

title: Action People-2.3: Indicators
text:
Full Action Statement: Develop and update indicators to track environmental and socioeconomic trends.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls NBEP and partners to develop and update indicators to track environmental and socioeconomic trends. Stressor indicators provide information about water quality, landscape and chemical processes that can cause changes in the estuaries and their watersheds. Condition indicators provide information about the status and trends of natural ecosystems and human socioeconomic systems, like public health (NBEP 2017).
NBEP convened more than 50 experts from universities, agencies, and organizations in Rhode Island and Massachusetts to develop environmental indicators and evaluate their status and trends. The resulting report identified and analyzed the status and trends of 24 key environmental and socioeconomic indicators (NBEP 2017). The geographic scope of most indicators was limited to Narragansett Bay and its watershed. Since the report’s publication, some indicators have been updated and expanded to include the Coastal Salt Ponds and Little Narragansett Bay. In addition, NBEP has refined three indicators to include new information, including Nuisance Flooding (Sea Level), Atmospheric Nitrogen Deposition (Nutrient Loading), and Solar Project Installations in Forests (Land Use) (Appendix A). NBEP continues to work with the Southeast New England Program to align indicator development to improve efficiency and create consistent messaging.
In 2023, NBEP’s Social Science Working Group nominated four new socioeconomic indicators for development, including Public Access, Resilient Municipalities, Public Health, and Economy. NBEP will develop and implement Public Access and Resilient Municipalities indicators over the next ten years (NBEP 2023a).
NBEP will lead development, metric refinement, and updates of environmental and socioeconomic indicators as guided by the Research Plan, Science Advisory Committee, Steering Committee, and topical work groups. NBEP will also expand the geographic scope of indicators to include Little Narragansett Bay and Rhode Island Coastal Ponds watersheds. Results will be published as an update to The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed report.


Potential Projects

Develop refined metrics, assess and update existing indicators, and create new environmental and socioeconomic indicators.
Expand the geographic scope of all indicators to include the Rhode Island Coastal Ponds and Little Narragansett Bay watersheds.
Update The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed report in 2027, emphasizing indicators that facilitate tracking CCMP goals and objectives.



Outputs

Indicator updates
New indicators, including socioeconomic indicators
Update of The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed report
Expanded geographic scope of indicators



Outcomes

Improved understanding of environmental stressors and conditions in the region
Improved capacity to track implementation of CCMP Actions
Improved understanding of human-ecosystem interactions



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Synthesize and communicate science


Implementing Partners
Science Advisory Committee, Southeast New England Program (SNEP), EPA Region 1, Other researchers


Location
Narragansett Bay region


Timeline for initiation
Ongoing


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$500K–1M
NBEP 320/SNEP

title: Action People-2.1: Science Communication Tools
text:
Full Action Statement: Develop compelling open-source data visualizations, stories, and dashboards.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will develop science communication to inform and engage partners and other interested people on estuary and watershed science and restoration.
Synthesizing and communicating estuarine and watershed science is foundational to protecting and restoring water quality and the ecological integrity of the Narragansett Bay region. Too often, science-based information is presented in dense academic literature and government reports, uses language that is not fully understood by non-scientists, and is in a form that is not available or useful to end users with the skills and agency to act. Additionally, information that is freely available may be out of date or incorrect and some information may only be accessible to a small number of people within an organization.
NBEP strives to develop accurate, understandable, accessible, and up-to-date communication tools for groups as diverse as scientists, managers, public officials, educators, and private citizens. NBEP also hosts current and past plans, documents, and datasets in its website’s Resource Library and GIS Data Hub. Using data visualizations and dashboards to display environmental status and trends is a simple and elegant way to accurately and broadly communicate information in a timely, accessible fashion. Narrative stories and StoryMaps are also effective tools. Creating these tools using open-source software that is freely available without a commercial license ensures that tools can be widely shared, modified, and accessible to all.
Through its roles to convene and synthesize and communicate science, NBEP will facilitate widespread adoption of open-source tools; collaborate with data developers, partners, and end users to develop tools that respond to current needs; and advertise new tools and develop training sessions for the tools, if necessary.


Potential Projects

Collaborate with local water quality monitoring groups and other National Estuary Programs to develop water quality dashboards that aid in analyzing and sharing data.
Create tool(s) to document and showcase habitat restoration projects in the region.
Build tool(s) to help local organizations prioritize areas for conservation or restoration.
Collaborate with data developers, NBEP committee experts, and end users to develop tools that respond to current needs.



Outputs

New tools
Ongoing maintenance and updates to existing tools



Outcomes

Greater ability for end users to understand technical issues and be proactive
Decreased technical debt and shorter, more efficient workflows for local organizations, leading to faster data analysis and availability



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Synthesize and communicate science


Implementing Partners
Steering Committee, Science Advisory Committee


Location
Narragansett Bay region


Timeline for initiation
Ongoing


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$10K–500K (NBEP staff time)
NBEP 320/SNEP

title: Action People-1.1: Engagement Plan
text:
Full Action Statement: Develop and implement the NBEP Engagement Plan to convene and activate partners to implement priority CCMP actions.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will develop a coordinated and collaborative NBEP Engagement Plan to guide NBEP’s convening services and partnership-building activities. The Engagement Plan will include work to articulate and promote a unique identity for the Narragansett Bay region that highlights specific CCMP implementation opportunities and challenges within the broader southern New England context (see Finance Plan Action: People-3.1).
Successful implementation of the NBEP CCMP requires an informed, engaged, and empowered community of partners making choices and taking actions that increase environmental health and resilience. Coproduction of knowledge and management solutions can build trust among resource users and managers and provide deeper understandings and diverse perspectives about what the people of the Narragansett Bay region value most about the environment. NBEP seeks to energize effective partnerships to implement CCMP actions and develop a strategic framework of networks and services to support partner needs for targeted engagement throughout the watershed.
NBEP will lead the development and regular update of the Engagement Plan to describe, prioritize, coordinate, and drive NBEP’s convening, outreach, education, and communication activities necessary to implement the CCMP and promote a unique regional identity. The Engagement Plan will guide meaningful collaboration with people and communities most impacted by pressing environmental issues, such as nonprofits, tribes, municipalities, academic researchers, community leaders, businesses, and more. It will also facilitate delivery of responsive, research-based outreach programs focused on priority topics.
The Engagement Plan will coordinate and amplify ongoing efforts and identify opportunities and gaps that can be met by existing or new programs and funding mechanisms. It will also broaden engagement approaches historically limited by organizational or political boundaries to address issues at environmentally meaningful watershed scales. The Engagement Plan will prioritize, focus, and empower engagement efforts to optimize collaboration and support for implementing CCMP objectives and actions. This coordinated approach will inspire new collaborations among existing programs, reinforce existing partnerships, and optimize messaging effectiveness.
Implementing the Engagement Plan requires NBEP to continue hosting quarterly Executive and Steering Committee meetings, biannual Science Advisory Committee meetings, and subject-specific subcommittees and working groups as needed. NBEP will also host topic-specific events, including the Annual Science Event, and offer convening services for partner-led workshops, committees, or meetings to implement partner strategic plans, workplans, or equivalent planning documents that align with the CCMP and the Engagement Plan. NBEP will regularly update the Engagement Plan to adapt to changing conditions in the environment and needs of the people in the region.


Potential Projects

Convene NBEP partners and other organizations to advance connections, knowledge, and solutions at local and regional scales.
Convene partners to inventory and coordinate needs for education and outreach programs.
Convene partners to explore avenues to establish and promote a unique identity for the Narragansett Bay region to support funding requests and public engagement.
Conduct NBEP Science Events.
Review existing local and state plans (e.g., Wildlife Action Plans, Nonpoint Source Pollution Management Plans) and compile lists of recommended policy topics that have potential for bi-state or interjurisdictional interest; then, obtain Steering Committee consensus on priority topics, next steps, and appropriate outputs.
Establish a regular schedule of communications with elected and appointed leaders at local, state, and federal levels to share NBEP and partner activities, accomplishments, and benefits.



Outputs

NBEP Engagement Plan and regular updates
Annual funding opportunities intended specifically for watershed outreach and education that implement the CCMP in the Narragansett Bay region
Events to promote engagement with priority science, preservation, and restoration issues and opportunities
Working group meetings
Reports on select policy topics



Outcomes

NBEP decisions, services, and projects that are responsive to current needs
Improved partner coordination for CCMP implementation at various scales (e.g., watershed, state, interstate)
Increased NBEP and partner ownership of progress toward CCMP goals and objectives
Public support for and participation in CCMP implementation
Adoption of policy changes that advance CCMP goals and objectives



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Fund capacity and projects


Implementing Partners
Steering Committee, other agencies and non-governmental organizations


Location
Narragansett Bay region, with priority areas to be determined during development and biannual update of the Engagement Plan


Timeline for initiation
Ongoing


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$1M–5M (NBEP staff time to develop Engagement Plan; annual RFPs for outreach and education activities)
NBEP 320/SNEP; NOAA B-WET, EPA, private foundations

title: About NBEP
text:
NBEP is a catalyst for scientific inquiry and collective action to restore and protect water quality, wildlife, and quality of life in the Narragansett Bay region, including Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, the Coastal Salt Ponds, and their watersheds. NBEP envisions clean water and habitat to sustain all who live, work and play in the Narragansett Bay region. Hosted by Roger Williams University (RWU), with a staff of five and governed by a 28-member Steering Committee, NBEP is the only partner-led organization pursuing place-based conservation across the three-state Narragansett Bay region of Rhode Island, Massachusetts, and Connecticut.
NBEP was one of the first four estuaries to be included in the National Estuary Program (NEP), which is now 28 members strong. The NEP, administered by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), was created by Congress through 1987 Amendments to the federal Clean Water Act. The enabling legislation was drafted by late Rhode Island Senator John H. Chafee and other leaders in Congress to protect and restore “estuaries of national significance.” The NEP designation provided funding and structure for the nascent Narragansett Bay Program to publish the results and recommendations from seven years of intensive study of Narragansett Bay and its watershed as the first Comprehensive Conservation and Management Plan (CCMP) for Narragansett Bay (1992). The 1992 CCMP laid the groundwork for NBEP to operate as a locally-led, collaborative, non-regulatory program focused on protecting and restoring the water quality and ecological integrity of Narragansett Bay. NBEP is one of six NEPs with watersheds in New England. While NBEP engages with all NEPs, NBEP coordinates particularly closely with neighboring Buzzards Bay National Estuary Program and Long Island Sound Partnership due to small study area overlaps (2.8 square miles and 75.6 square miles, respectively). Staff carefully communicate with each other about funded projects and other work within the overlap areas to ensure appropriate levels of support across all communities within NEP regions.

title: About NBEP
text:
Collaborative Planning
NBEP pursues independent convening, planning, and consensus-building among diverse groups of partners and communities to address shared issues and opportunities. Key examples of NBEP’s collaborative planning work include:

The Blackstone Needs Assessment project, in which NBEP convened Blackstone River Watershed interests to explore local needs, develop the Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report (NBEP 2021a), and establish a local coordinating organization to lead implementation of the report. As of this document’s publication, the Blackstone Watershed Collaborative is an independent nonprofit organization operating as an impact network to promote learning and coordinate action to solve complex watershed issues that no one organization could solve on its own. This work also provided a replicable model for creating the pre-conditions necessary to advance paired ecosystem and community health in other large and complex watersheds in the Narragansett Bay region.
The Lower Blackstone Fish Passage Project, in which NBEP led partners to reinvigorate progress on efforts to establish diadromous fish passage across four dams on the lower Blackstone River in Pawtucket, Rhode Island. In 2022, NBEP transitioned to a funder role and now supports the capacity of lead partners to continue convening, advocacy, and planning work on the largest fish passage project in the Narragansett Bay watershed with over $500,000 of NBEP’s Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds allocated to date.
Science Working Groups that convene partners to investigate and respond to ongoing and emerging topics, including:

Salt Marsh Restoration, Assessment, and Monitoring Program (RAMP): Established in 2020 to formalize a decades-long cooperation between multiple federal, state, local, non-profit, and academic agencies to restore, assess, and monitor salt marshes throughout Rhode Island.
Social Science Working Group: Established in 2020 to advance the integration of current social science into environmental decision-making in the Narragansett Bay region.
Fishermen’s Ecological Knowledge Working Group: Established in 2021 to create a collaborative multidisciplinary team to assess how best to integrate fishermen’s observations into existing ecological monitoring frameworks.
Seaweed Ecology and Assessment Collaborative (SEAC-RI): Established in 2024 to understand the role macroalgae plays in the ecosystem and how it impacts seagrasses, oyster beds, and other habitats throughout the region.
Submerged Aquatic Vegetation Working Group: Established in 2020 and revitalized in 2025 to advance seagrass monitoring and restoration in Rhode Island waters.
Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative: Established in 2024 to provide cross-state collaboration and coordination to understand ecosystem change in urban coastal waters informing resource management, resilience, and habitat improvement.

title: About NBEP
text:
Science Synthesis and Communication
NBEP provides scientific data analysis and communication using innovative storytelling and open-source web application tools to investigate complex socioenvironmental topics. NBEP serves as a hub for information-sharing tools such as:

2017 State of Narragansett Bay and its Watershed Report: This technical report, co-authored by NBEP and partners, describes 24 environmental and socioeconomic condition and stressor indicators that assess Narragansett Bay and its watershed (NBEP 2017). Since publication, NBEP has updated numerous indicators with more recent data, added new focus areas to existing indicators, and expanded indicators to cover the entire Narragansett Bay region to include the Rhode Island Coastal Ponds, Little Narragansett Bay, and their respective watersheds (See Appendix A).
A publicly-accessible GIS Data Hub that facilitates data-sharing and hosts interactive maps and StoryMaps to dive into complex topics, including:

How Do We Use Our Coasts? StoryMap (NBEP 2021): NBEP, alongside partners at US EPA, used anonymized cell phone visitation data to assess usage of public coastal access sites.
Hundred Acre Cove Septic Viewer and Stormwater Infrastructure Dashboard (Save The Bay 2021a): NBEP, in partnership with Save The Bay, created GIS tools to inform actions to better protect water quality in Hundred Acre Cove in the tidal Barrington River.
Clean Water and Quahogs StoryMap (NBEP 2022): NBEP created an interactive tool to explain and celebrate the conditional opening of 1,900 acres of the lower Providence River for shellfishing for the first time in 75 years.
Into the Woods: Solar in the Forest StoryMap (NBEP 2024): NBEP created a tool to visualize the number and location of ground-mounted solar projects in the Narragansett Bay region and assess how much undeveloped land has been lost to solar development across the region and at subwatershed scales.

Open-source data visualization tools:

WQDashboard (2026): NBEP built a web tool using the Shiny application for R to enable local watershed groups to display water quality data on their websites. As of 2026, the tool has been adopted by the Blackstone River Coalition and the Salt Ponds Coalition.
MarshMap (2025): At the request of the Salt Marsh RAMP Working Group, NBEP built a tool that displays information about salt marsh restoration and monitoring projects in Rhode Island.

title: About NBEP
text:
Project Funding
NBEP provides watershed project funding that helps build a pipeline of future projects that restore and sustain the natural environment. Funding supports research, staff capacity, and project planning steps, including studies, assessments, and engineering design. NBEP’s far-reaching outreach to partners between 2019–2021 identified a lack of local capacity in state agencies, municipalities, and non-governmental organizations as the single biggest barrier to new project development in the Narragansett Bay region. In response, NBEP developed a nine-step Project Development Process (Figure 2) that aims to communicate the importance of often-underfunded pre-project development steps, from engagement to design and planning. As a result of this outreach and the significant influx of Infrastructure and Jobs Act funds beginning in 2022, NBEP cemented its niche as a funder of pre-construction engagement and planning. While the sunset of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds will impact NBEP’s ability to support project development, this CCMP Revision lays the foundation for work to secure alternative sustainable funding sources.






Figure 2: NBEP Project Development Process



Since 2019, NBEP has granted over $5 million to priority restoration, research, and community engagement projects (Figure 3), including:

Green Infrastructure Grants (2021, 2022): NBEP awarded $953,988 in §320 funds for stormwater planning projects that align natural and engineering processes to improve water quality and reduce flooding while offering a range of co-benefits, including resilience to increasing rainfall intensity, wildlife habitat, public health, and other socioeconomic and community enhancements.
Capacity-Building Grants (2022, 2023): NBEP awarded over $1 million in §320 and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds to support staff capacity within seven partner organizations. To date, recipients have more than doubled the amount awarded to them by NBEP in additional grant leverage. The early results of this grant program demonstrate the power of supporting people and organizations that already have relationships and agency to push projects forward.
Habitat Restoration and Public Access Grants (2023): NBEP awarded $748,822 in Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds for seven planning projects to improve watershed habitats and public access to those habitats. Project benefits include improving marsh resilience and creating more waterfront public recreational access points while restoring coastal habitat and improving flood resilience. Grants help fund the critical early pre-construction phases of project planning and design.
Public Outreach and Education Grants (2021–2025): NBEP awarded $167,086 in §320 funds for eleven outreach and education projects seeking to meaningfully involve watershed residents in restoration. From school programs, to work-force development, to volunteer programs and boater outreach, these grants help partners directly educate and engage the public on key watershed issues.
Aquatic Habitat Connectivity Grants (2022, 2024, 2025): NBEP awarded over $2.25 million in Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds for capacity-building and planning projects that seek to remove barriers to aquatic organism passage, including dam removals, culvert upgrades, and fish passage structures. These projects help support important fisheries and better protect communities from flooding.







Figure 3: Projects funded by NBEP, 2012 - 2024

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text: This 2026 CCMP Revision is a regional call to action for shared efforts that can measurably improve the region’s water quality, wildlife, and quality of life over the next decade. Rather than a comprehensive list of the many ongoing initiatives that will benefit the region’s estuaries and watersheds, this 2026 CCMP Revision contains a targeted set of priority Actions for collaborative work among NBEP staff, organizations represented on NBEP’s committees, and other partners throughout the watershed. The list of priority Actions was crafted using a cooperative process that reflects best available science.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Regionalism
Actions leverage the partnership’s unique geopolitical identity as the Narragansett Bay region to build regional partnerships for collaboration and cross-boundary coordination.
Since the publication of the 2012 CCMP, several interstate watershed groups focused on the Taunton, Blackstone, and Wood-Pawcatuck watersheds have gained strength, visibility, and agency to pursue priorities at watershed scales. The SNEP Network, which provides technical assistance services to communities throughout Southern New England, emerged in 2019 as a crucial support and networking tool for communities seeking assistance with watershed planning. NBEP and partners will build upon these strengths to continue providing services to Massachusetts and Rhode Island partners while seeking to establish new partnerships with communities and organizations in the Little Narragansett Bay watershed in Connecticut.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Connectivity
Actions reflect the importance of connection—people to people, people to natural spaces, watersheds to estuaries, and wildlife to their habitats—by protecting access, removing barriers, and facilitating dialogue.
Habitat connectivity is a prime focus area for NBEP over the next ten years. Priorities include facilitating and funding progress on reconnecting rivers and streams from their headwaters to their receiving estuaries and connecting coastal marshes to uplands to facilitate marsh migration with rising seas. Local watershed groups have gained capacity in recent years and are in good position to continue driving connectivity projects that respond specifically to needs in their watersheds. Regional salt marsh working groups, including the Salt Marsh RAMP and the Massachusetts Ecosystem Climate Adaptation Network (Mass ECAN) Salt Marsh Working Group, have collaboratively pioneered techniques for restoring and reconnecting subsiding and drowning marshes and are working together to coordinate restoration and monitoring of large projects.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Resiliency
Actions are responsive and resilient to extreme weather events, including more intense precipitation events, storm surge intensification, and higher sea levels. These impacts exacerbate coastal and rain-driven flooding while eroding coastlines, riverbanks, and marshes.
States in the Narragansett Bay region are taking lead roles in planning and funding projects to increase the region’s resilience. The Massachusetts Municipal Vulnerability Preparedness (MVP) Program, first piloted in the Taunton River watershed, was established statewide in 2017 and has since 2018 provided over $15M to communities in the Massachusetts portion of the Narragansett Bay region to increase their resiliency through vulnerability assessments and project planning. Rhode Island’s Municipal Resilience Program (MRP) has granted over $18M to cities and towns between 2020–2024 to complete municipal-driven workshop processes and implement priority projects.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Fairness
Actions strive to deliver fair distribution of benefits.
Since the publication of the 2012 CCMP, NBEP and partners have worked to plan for fairer distribution of environmental programs and benefits. For example, NBEP has intentionally invited more Massachusetts representatives to participate in its committees and working groups. As a result, NBEP has supported more projects and organizations in the region’s tributaries and watersheds in addition to projects in Narragansett Bay proper. Furthermore, NBEP has worked to engage new partners through grant programs, innovative outreach and convening, and research initiatives that elevate fishers and shellfishers, Tribes, and grassroots organizations.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Anatomy of an Action Brief

Action Description and Statement of Need – what partners and NBEP staff will do and why
Potential Projects – specific actionable tasks needed to implement the action plan
Outputs – products or services resulting from the action
Outcomes – system changes expected from completing the action
State of the Watershed Indicators – metrics by which change will be measured
Role of NBEP – specifies the service(s) that NBEP staff will provide: convene, synthesize and communicate science, fund capacity and projects
Implementing Partners – organizations that can lead or assist with action implementation due to their statutory authority, expertise, and anticipated work activities
Location – specific watershed location, if any, where the action will be directed
Timeline for Initiation – time frames for initiating the action: ongoing (initiated before 2025), short-term (1–3 years 2026–2029), medium-term (4–6 years 2030–2032), long-term (7–10 years 2033–2036)
Estimated Costs – costs for all partners over 10 years estimated at five levels (2024 dollars):

$10K–500K (NBEP staff time, capacity-building and outreach grants)
$500K–1M (convene partners, support research/modeling projects)
$1M–5M (develop and implement regional plans)
$5M–10M (project plans - designs and permits)
$10M+ (infrastructure and restoration construction)

Potential Funding Sources – organizations or grants with potential to fund projects
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy
text:
Supporting Documents
Implementation of the CCMP Strategy is supported by several overarching NBEP guidance documents:

The State of Narragansett Bay and Its Watershed (2017, to be updated 2027) details the status and trends for a suite of 24 stressor and condition indicators and provides insights into the ecosystem health and resilience of the Narragansett Bay region.
NBEP Research Plan (2024) created the Urban Waters Research Collaborative and guides NBEP’s scientific program, including setting targets for funding research to implement the CCMP.
NBEP Engagement Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will specify guiding principles and strategies to convene and activate partners and key audiences on priority environmental issues and promote a regional identity.
NBEP Habitat Restoration Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will outline opportunities to protect and restore key watershed and estuarine habitats.
NBEP Finance Plan (to be adopted by 2029) will describe the strategy for NBEP’s long-term financial sustainability as well as catalyze fundraising and investments for projects that implement the CCMP.
crumbs: About the CCMP Vision and Strategy

title: About the Narragansett Bay Region
text:
The Narragansett Bay region spans over 2,000 square miles of land comprising the watersheds of Narragansett Bay, Little Narragansett Bay, and the Coastal Salt Ponds. This landscape unites two million people across 113 cities and towns in Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Connecticut. A mosaic of urban and suburban lands is surrounded by forested landscapes and networks of rivers and streams. Reaching inland more than 50 miles, the region’s watersheds have over 4,000 miles of rivers and streams and over 40,000 acres of lakes and ponds. Salty shores stretch along 700 miles of dunes, beaches, and rocky coasts. The region hosts diverse habitats, including roughly 4,300 acres of salt marsh habitat and 875 acres of seagrass habitat, that sustain wildlife, support regional economies, and provide recreational opportunities.

Narragansett Bay Watershed
The Narragansett Bay watershed spans 1,705 square miles across Rhode Island and Massachusetts and includes the major tributaries of the Blackstone River, Taunton River, and Pawtuxet River that flow into the 196 square mile bay. In Narragansett Bay, salt marshes and seagrasses have declined considerably from historical extents. Fish communities are transitioning toward seasonal and mid-Atlantic species as Bay water temperatures rise. Storms have eroded Bay shorelines. Upland in the watershed, development pressure is a major stressor, causing loss of forested and wetland habitats, impacting wildlife and the health of inland and coastal waters. The watershed is fourteen percent impervious cover and half of rivers and streams are unhealthy for fish and wildlife due to excess nutrients, oxygen depletion, and other parameters (NBEP 2017). Thousands of barriers, including dams and improperly sized culverts, not only block migration for important diadromous fish populations, but also increase flood risk. These impacts are felt particularly in the urban areas of Providence, Worcester, Fall River, and Woonsocket, where increasingly intense rainfall events flood roads, homes, and businesses. Decades of efforts have dramatically reduced nutrient and bacteria pollution in the Bay, yet half of the estuarine waters are still unhealthy for fish and other aquatic life, with stormwater pollution driving the majority of impairments (NBEP 2017).


Little Narragansett Bay Watershed
The Little Narragansett Bay watershed spans 317 square miles across western Rhode Island and southeastern Connecticut and includes the major tributaries of the Wood River and Pawcatuck River that flow into a 4.8 square mile estuary. Compared to the Narragansett Bay watershed, the Little Narragansett Bay watershed has one-fifth the population density and is more heavily forested and protected with conservation lands. The Barn Island Wildlife Management Area—the largest managed coastal habitat area in Connecticut—protects substantial areas of salt marsh, deciduous forest, and avian habitat. Only five percent of the watershed is impervious cover and over seventy percent of the assessed streams and rivers are fit for aquatic life (NBEP 2017). Increasingly, the major watershed stressor is development-driven changes in land use. Water quality impacts tend to be concentrated near these expanding developed areas. Within the bay proper, seagrasses remain scarce and shellfishing is prohibited due to water quality concerns.


Coastal Salt Ponds Watersheds
The Coastal Salt Ponds watersheds span 56 square miles along the southern coast of Rhode Island, running from Point Judith in the east to Little Narragansett Bay in the west. The Rhode Island Coastal Salt Ponds—Judith Pond, Potter’s Pond, Cards Pond, Trustom Pond, Greenhill Pond, Ninigret Pond, Quonochontaug Pond, Winnapaug Pond, and Maschaug Pond—function as coastal estuarine lagoons connected to the ocean by inlets or breachways through barrier beaches. The ponds are shallow and brackish, which make them valuable as fish and shellfish nurseries but also susceptible to eutrophication and bacterial loading. Each pond has a unique identity and supports iconic wildlife and coastal habitats as well as many important commercial and recreational activities, including aquaculture. Pond assessments show 85% are fit for aquatic wildlife and 100% are fit for recreation (NBEP 2017). The long stretches of adjacent sandy beaches draw locals and visitors alike, making them important recreational and economic resources. Population size and development have grown across the salt ponds region in the last twenty years, though the population density is still only half that of the Narragansett Bay watershed. Rising sea levels and altered hydrology are major stressors for these unique coastal salt pond habitats causing erosion and subsidence that outpace marsh accretion.

title: Action People-1.2: Research Plan
text:
Full Action Statement: Implement the NBEP Research Plan and Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will implement the NBEP Research Plan (NBEP 2024) to synthesize existing research and, with the guidance of the Science Advisory Committee, prioritize new topics for NBEP investment and identify key knowledge gaps.
The Research Plan unites the scientific interests of NBEP partners to meet the research needs of the CCMP and inform management decisions. Implementation of the Research Plan will:

Further develop metrics and update indicators of environmental and socioeconomic conditions (Action: People-2.3)
Support, improve, and create efficiencies for ecosystem modeling to answer complex questions and improve ecosystem management (Action: People-2.2)
Create shared ecosystem restoration targets that span boundaries (Action: People-3.2)

The Research Plan responds to NBEP Science Advisory Committee (SAC) priorities, including synthesis of existing data, collection of baseline and new data, analysis and modeling, and information sharing. A new Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaboration, composed of NBEP Science Advisory Committee (SAC) members, researchers, managers, and local experts, will focus on the region’s coastal urban sub-watersheds Hydrologic Unit Code 12 (HUC12). The initial focus is on the Providence-Seekonk Rivers, which continue to exhibit water quality concerns including low dissolved oxygen and elevated pathogens. Over the next ten years, as resources allow, NBEP will expand the collaborations into Fall River and the Westerly-Stonington areas.
NBEP will fund implementation of the Research Plan through requests for proposals, direct awards, and contracts.


Potential Projects

Convene Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative focused on the Providence River Estuary, and as time allows, additional focus areas.
Conduct literature reviews of the three priority coastal urban centers (Providence, Fall River, and Westerly) to identify data resources, seminal papers, and links to other reviews and analyses.
Compile and improve information sharing on planned and completed research related to the NBEP region.
Develop requests for proposals or direct awards to implement the Research Plan.



Outputs

Ongoing meetings of the Coastal Urban Waters Research Collaborative
Requests for proposals that advance research needs identified in the Research Plan
Biennial updates to the Research Plan, including evaluations of Plan implementation



Outcomes

Articulated research needs to advance NBEP priorities
Enhanced interdisciplinary networks of researchers, managers, and local people



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Synthesize and communicate science, Fund capacity and projects


Implementing Partners
Science Advisory Committee and other researchers from management agencies, NGOs, and academia


Location
Narragansett Bay region, focusing on the coastal urban sub-watersheds (HUC12 scale)


Timeline for initiation
Ongoing


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$5M–10M
NBEP 320/SNEP, Rhode Island Sea Grant, NOAA, NSF, private foundations

title: Action People-2.2: Ecosystem Modeling and Decision-Support Tools
text:
Full Action Statement: Support, improve, and create efficiencies for integrated ecosystem models and decision-support tools that foster effective resource management.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action will advance the development and use of ecosystem modeling and decision-support tools for filling knowledge gaps, informing key management decisions, and setting and tracking progress toward environmental protection and restoration targets.
Significant environmental research and monitoring has occurred in the Narragansett Bay region since at least the 1950s which has fueled multiple modeling efforts to understand how ecosystems respond to pollution drivers and management actions (Humphries et al. 2022). Applied models focus on answering key management-related questions related to water quality, food-chain dynamics, fisheries resources, and larger ecosystem-based challenges. Localized needs have been met with models and other tools that support TMDL development and permitting, prioritize restoration projects, and highlight gaps in current monitoring activities.
There has long been a desire for linked hydrodynamic, water quality, and ecosystem-based models to help increase understanding of the impact of system stressors and responsive management actions. Using monitoring data in models can assist in forecasting future conditions, selecting responsive actions, tracking their effectiveness, and adapting as needed. Ecosystem models can be used to compare multiple future ecosystem scenarios and identify management strategies that sustain benefits for ecosystem, people, and economies across various time scales and uncertainties.
The rising and interlaced challenges of nutrient loading, stormwater pollution, and socioeconomic concerns necessitate the revision, update, and creation of models and other decision-support tools to better understand ecosystem health, evaluate management strategies, and help set and track progress against management targets. Further, not all models or tools are in forms that are available or useful to end users. Data visualizations and dashboards are simple and elegant ways to accurately and broadly communicate information to a wide audience of partners and other end users. Creating tools using free open-source software ensures that they can be widely shared, modified, and accessible to all.
NBEP will collaborate with partners to convene interests across state boundaries to coordinate modeling and tool development. Activities could include assessing current modeling and tool-development projects to avoid duplication of efforts, discussing gaps in monitoring needed to accurately develop or update models and tools, and gaining consensus on a preferred pathway to developing a bay-wide water quality model. NBEP will participate on partner-led committees to develop or improve models or tools. NBEP can partially or fully fund development, update, or implementation of open-source models or tools to answer key management questions. NBEP will regularly provide guidance and updates to ensure modeling and tool development aligns with CCMP actions.


Potential Projects

Convene stakeholders and other experts to review available modeling tools and their limitations.
Convene partners to build consensus on pathways for development of validated and calibrated bay water quality and ecosystem models to support management and assist in setting restoration targets.
Collaborate with data developers, NBEP committee experts, and end users to develop technical tools that respond to current needs.
Develop and/or update natural heritage database models to inform land use decisions.



Outputs

Assessment of existing water quality and hydrodynamic models, their intended use, and a pathway toward a larger, linked hydrodynamic model
Assessment of data needs for existing models and tools
Working group meetings



Outcomes

More efficient and accurate modeling tools for decision-making and policy
Broader understanding of available modeling tools and their applicability to resource management
Ecosystem management that is better informed by modeling and monitoring data



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Fund capacity and projects


Implementing Partners
RIDEM, MassDEP, URI Coastal Institute, RI Environmental Monitoring Collaborative, Rhode Island Natural History Survey, NBNERR, Mass Audubon, MassWildlife, TNC (RI and MA), ASRI, Salt Ponds Coalition, URI Watershed Watch, Save The Bay, Brown University, URI, EPA-ORD-ACESD


Location
Narragansett Bay region


Timeline for Initiation
2030-2032


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$5M–10M
NBEP 320/SNEP, National Science Foundation, Foundation Grants, URI Coastal Institute, RIDEM, MassDEP, TNC
crumbs: Action People-2.2: Ecosystem Modeling and Decision-Support Tools

title: Action People-3.1: Finance Plan
text:
Full Action Statement: Develop the NBEP Finance Plan to catalyze fundraising and investments for projects that implement the CCMP.

Action Description and Statement of Need
This Action calls NBEP to lead development of a Finance Plan to 1) ensure NBEP secures sufficient funding to provide its core services, 2) guide NBEP’s funding decisions on a five to ten-year timescale, and 3) convene efforts to increase funding for priority restoration projects.
In addition to consistent funding through the National Estuary Program, watershed protection efforts in the Narragansett Bay region benefit from an EPA geographic program (the Southeast New England Program (SNEP)) and state funding for conservation, restoration, and resilience projects. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act dollars allocated to NBEP and partners between 2022–2025 have provided essential support for staff capacity, project development, and project implementation.
Since 2022, NBEP’s staff has grown from two to five full-time employees. This growth has enabled NBEP to increase and diversify services that it provides to partners and other organizations, including convening, science communication, and project funding. With the recent hires of two new positions, Watershed Outreach Manager and Staff Ecologist, NBEP anticipates further diversifying its services to include direct outreach and habitat project implementation. The NBEP Finance Plan will seek to balance NBEP’s internal operations requirements with external subawards and contracts to support CCMP implementation.
Due to realized and anticipated reductions in federal investments in conservation efforts over early implementation of this CCMP Revision, it is important for NBEP to develop a targeted strategy for granting subawards and contracts. NBEP will work closely with partners during development of the Finance Plan to identify the highest-priority projects in this CCMP Revision to pursue with external awards within the framework of NBEP’s nine-step Project Development Process (Figure 4).
Despite decades of consistent—and in recent years, increased—federal and state financial support for restoration, project funding remains insufficient. The Narragansett Bay region receives roughly one-fifth of the federal dollars per square mile that are received to support comparable project needs in Chesapeake Bay, Puget Sound, Long Island Sound, and Lake Champlain regions. Through development and implementation of its Finance Plan and Engagement Plan (Action: People-1.1), NBEP will convene partners to seek additional funding for water quality and habitat restoration.


Potential Projects

Convene partners to identify the highest-priority projects in this CCMP Revision to pursue with external awards within the framework of NBEP’s nine-step Project Development Process.
Develop an NBEP Finance Plan and seek approval by the Steering Committee and EPA Region 1.
Solicit and award funding for staffing, planning, design, and construction of water quality, habitat, and public access projects.



Outputs

NBEP Finance Plan
NBEP project funding solicitations
NBEP project awards
Funded planning and construction projects



Outcomes

Increased collaborative and strategic fundraising
Increased awareness of the cost, value, and importance of conservation, restoration, and resilience efforts in the Narragansett Bay region
Increased regional staff capacity for project planning, development, implementation, and maintenance
Increased number of conservation, restoration, and resilience projects in the region
Improved ecosystem and community health and resilience



Indicators
All indicators


NBEP role
Convene, Fund capacity and projects


Implementing Partners
Steering Committee


Location
Narragansett Bay region


Timeline for initiation
2026


Estimated costs/potential funding sources
$10K–500K
NBEP 320/SNEP

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Vision
Informed and engaged partners are empowered to implement the CCMP.


Goal
Provide foundational knowledge, capacity, and drivers for CCMP implementation.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Engagement
Implementation of the NBEP CCMP requires informed, engaged, and empowered partners contributing collectively to shared goals. NBEP will build on its track record as a neutral convener and “honest broker” by developing a regional Engagement Plan to prioritize and focus NBEP’s engagement with partners and priority audiences (Action: People-1.1).

Public Engagement Highlight: Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report





NBEP convened a broad group of Blackstone River Watershed interests between 2019 and 2021 to explore local needs. The resulting Blackstone River Watershed Needs Assessment Report (NBEP 2021a) describes high-priority needs and 20 recommended actions. The first recommendation in the assessment, creation of the Blackstone Watershed Collaborative and hiring of a manager to oversee it, was accomplished in 2021 with NBEP funding. The Blackstone Watershed Collaborative has to date leveraged over $1M in other funding sources to continue its operation and support of small organizations. This project enabled the Blackstone River to join Narragansett Bay’s other major tributary, the Taunton River, in having an up-to-date plan and coordinating organization to lead its implementation.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Information and Tracking
NBEP consistently tracks and reports on the status and trends of ecological conditions of greatest concern for management and public interest. Monitoring, research, data synthesis, and modeling are necessary to ensure that NBEP’s reporting is based on the best available science.
In 2024, NBEP and partners developed a Research Plan to coordinate and foster research necessary to better understand ecosystem change, improve aquatic habitats, and advance work on environmental indicators. Implementation of the NBEP Research Plan (Action: People-1.2) will increase information and understanding to inform, guide, and evaluate management actions. Advancing and increasing use of integrated ecosystem data synthesis and modeling tools (Action: People-2.2) will better equip scientists and managers to make decisions and better understand the complex interactions between water quality, ecosystems, and socioeconomic indicators.
In 2003, NBEP and partners developed a suite of environmental indicators for Narragansett Bay and its watershed (NBEP 2003). In 2017, the list was reviewed and expanded to 24 stressor and condition indicators that underlie ecosystem and socioeconomic health (NBEP 2017). Since then, indicators have been updated with new data and focus areas (Appendix A). There is a continued need to refine and improve metrics of existing indicators and develop new environmental and socioeconomic indicators to address priority management issues that may arise (Action: People-2.3).
NBEP strives to communicate effectively with partners and the broader interested public by developing accurate, understandable, and accessible science communication products for different user groups (Action: People-2.1). Data visualizations and dashboards are effective tools to display information about environmental status and trends. Narrative stories, GIS StoryMaps, and digestible fact sheets also translate complex science for broad readership and encourage further inquiry. Information provided in clear, accessible formats can assist resource managers, non-governmental organizations, decision-makers, and others to better understand stressors affecting ecosystem health and to identify how environmental conditions are changing and impacting communities. These communications are foundational for building public support for action and informing management decisions, including strategic investment in protection and restoration.

Information and Tracking Highlight: Narragansett Bay Watershed Economy Report





The Narragansett Bay Watershed Economy Report (Uchida et al. 2019) was a collaborative effort that identified 13 key sectors that rely on the watershed’s natural capital and the cumulative economic impact of those sectors ($14B, 2016 dollars).
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Regional Cooperation
The Narragansett Bay region requires more funding for conservation, restoration, and resilience projects—and more staff capacity for project development and maintenance—to implement CCMP goals and objectives. Development of the NBEP Finance Plan will ensure NBEP secures sufficient funding to provide its core services and increase funding for CCMP priority projects within the framework of its nine-step Project Development Process (Action: People-3.1).
Once the status and trends of key environmental and socioeconomic indicators are determined, the community must reach consensus on measurable targets for those indicators that equate to a desired future condition (Action: People-3.2).

Governance Highlight: Massachusetts Division of Ecological Restoration Expansion





The Massachusetts Division of Ecological Restoration (MA DER) was established in 2009 as part of the Massachusetts Department of Fish and Game under the Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs to bring attention, momentum, and capacity to river and wetland restoration. Since then, MA DER and partners have completed more than 160 restoration projects, with over 80 active projects currently underway. Today, MA DER operates three branches (Capacity Building, Habitat Restoration, and Technical Services) and multiple funding programs that support local organizations and project development to improve habitat, reduce flooding, and improve water quality and public safety.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan

title: People Ready to Act Action Plan
text:
Resiliency Considerations
Higher sea levels, warmer water temperatures, and more intense precipitation and storm surge events will create additional challenges for managing natural, built, and socioeconomic systems in the Narragansett Bay region. Empowering partners and communities with the agency and tools to engage on issues related to regional vulnerability, adaptation, and mitigation are key to expanding resilience. Actions in the People Ready to Act Action Plan develop tools designed to empower partners and communities to take proactive action, advocate for decisions, understand the latest science, and identify management strategies that sustain multiple benefits across various time scales and uncertainties.
crumbs: People Ready to Act Action Plan
